1:
The chapter opens with a foreign queen drawn from the ends of the earth by a king's fame, and the witnesses read her journey as at once history and parable. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, noting that the episode had already been told at length in 1 Kings 10, will not pass it over, because our Saviour himself proposed the queen of the south as a pattern for all who seek him in Matthew 12:42. From her coming Henry draws his first lesson: those who honour God, God will honour, as 1 Samuel 2:30 promises. Solomon had greatly honoured God in building, beautifying, and dedicating the temple, laying out all his wisdom and wealth to make it a consummate piece; and now God made that wisdom and wealth redound to his reputation, so that the way to have both the credit and the comfort of all our endowments is to consecrate them to God and use them for him.

Henry's second lesson is the queen's own. Those who know the worth of true wisdom will grudge no pains nor cost to obtain it; she put herself to great trouble and expense to hear Solomon, yet, learning from him to serve God and do her duty, thought herself well paid, for heavenly wisdom is that pearl of great price which is a good bargain though we part with all we have, the merchantman's prize of Matthew 13:46. Her hard questions he takes not for idle curiosities but for the earnest enquiries of a soul that prized understanding above gold.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to how the chronicler has handled his source. Where the older account had the queen come concerning the name of Jehovah, our writer omits that difficult phrase; the hard questions he glosses as riddles or enigmas, the word the Septuagint uses of Samson's riddle in Judges 14:12; and for the very much gold of Kings the chronicler substitutes his own favourite expression, gold in abundance. The abridgment, Ellicott judges, is not always an improvement, but it is everywhere the mark of a later writer recopying and lightly reshaping an older record. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the chapter's whole course — the visit, the riches, the golden targets and ivory throne, the navy, the political greatness, the king's death — and refers the reader for the particulars of the royal visit to the fuller notice in 1 Kings 10, adding that the Targum styles her the queen of Zemargad. The witnesses do not divide: Henry hears in the verse a summons to prize and pursue wisdom, while Ellicott and Clarke fix the text and the history on which that summons rests.
2:
"And Solomon told her all her questions: and there was nothing hid from Solomon which he told her not." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens on the king's readiness to teach as the crown of his wisdom. As every man has received the gift, he says, so he ought to minister the same for the edification of others as he has opportunity; and Solomon was communicative of his wisdom, willing to teach others what he knew himself. Being taught of God, freely he had received, and freely he gave — the very rule the Lord lays upon his disciples in Matthew 10:8. Let those that are rich in wisdom, as well as in wealth, learn to do good and be ready to distribute, giving to every one that asks. The wisest of men did not hoard his understanding as a private treasure but spent it freely on a stranger's questions, hiding nothing she was able to receive.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward, setting down the queen's own testimony in full: that Solomon resolved all that was in her mind, so that nothing was too hard for him to declare; that when she had seen his wisdom and the house he had built, the meat of his table, the sitting of his servants and the attendance of his ministers in their apparel, and his ascent by which he went up to the house of the LORD, there was no more spirit left in her. The bare recital is itself a kind of commentary: the chronicler heaps detail upon detail until the visitor is overwhelmed, and the historian is content to let that mounting wonder testify to the greatness of the wisdom God had given.

What both witnesses honour, each in his way, is the union of fulness and freeness in Solomon's answer. Henry weighs the freeness — a wisdom given by God and therefore given away without grudging; Keil and Delitzsch display the fulness — a wisdom that met every riddle the queen had carried from her own land and left nothing unresolved. That "there was nothing hid from Solomon" marks the completeness of the gift he had begged and been granted, when he asked only for understanding to go out and come in before the people in 2 Chronicles 1:10. The king who sought no riches is now proved able to satisfy the hardest questions of the wisest of the Gentiles; and the scene, set at the threshold of the account of his glory, roots that glory in the wisdom he had of God and shows it overflowing, unhoarded, to bless even those who came from afar to seek it.
4:
The verse completes the inventory that took the queen's breath away — "the meat of his table, and the sitting of his servants, and the attendance of his ministers, and their apparel; his cupbearers also, and their apparel; and his ascent by which he went up into the house of the LORD; there was no more spirit in her." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the whole catalogue as a lesson in the beauty of good order. Good order in a great family, he says, especially in the things of God, and a regular discharge of the duties of religious worship, are highly to be admired wherever found; and the queen was exceedingly affected to see the propriety with which Solomon's servants attended him, and with which both he and they attended in the house of God. What overcame her was not luxury only but decorum — a court and a worship ordered to the last cupbearer's garment. Henry sets beside it the psalmist's own delight in going up to the house of the LORD in solemn procession, the pleasant memory of Psalms 42:4.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over that last item, the king's "ascent by which he went up into the house of the LORD," and finds a textual puzzle in it. The parallel in Kings reads not "his ascent" but "his burnt offering which he offered in the house of the LORD"; the Septuagint, Syriac, and Vulgate agree with Kings, and the Arabic reads "the altar on which he offered." In every other passage the Hebrew word here used means not an ascent but an upper chamber; it is likely, therefore, Ellicott concludes, that in this instance it is merely an error of transcription for the very similar term found in Kings, the word for burnt-offering. The two readings stand close in the unpointed Hebrew, and a copyist's eye could easily pass from the one to the other.

The witnesses do not contend, and their two labours meet on the same object. Whether the queen's wonder was crowned by the sight of the king mounting in state to the temple, as our text has it, or by the burnt-offering he presented there, as the older record and the versions suggest, the thing that emptied her of spirit was Solomon at worship — the wisest and wealthiest of kings going up, with all his ordered household, to the house of God. Ellicott secures the letter and weighs the versions; Henry draws the devotion, that a well-ordered service of God is itself a spectacle able to astonish a watching world, and that the true magnificence of Solomon's court was seen not at his table but on the road to the sanctuary.
5:
The queen finds her voice, and it is the voice of a convert to a truth she had doubted — "It was a true report which I heard in mine own land of thine acts, and of thy wisdom." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins, as ever, with the word. "Of thine acts" is literally "of thy words," the term the Septuagint renders in the same way; we might as well translate it "matters" or "affairs," so that her confession embraces both what Solomon had said and what he had done. The report she had received in her distant country concerned his sayings and his deeds together, and now, face to face, she owns it true.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in her admission the whole moral of the visit. She had come a long and costly way on the strength of a rumour, and she does not repent the journey; the report proved no exaggeration but an understatement, and the traveller who ventured much to hear wisdom finds herself well repaid. Henry's larger point holds here as it did over her coming: those who know the worth of true wisdom will grudge no pains to obtain it, and she who had staked her journey on a report is not ashamed to say aloud that the report was true. There is a candour in the confession that becomes her — she does not flatter, but attests; she measures the fame against the man and finds the man the greater.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the queen's speech, set down the full testimony of which this is the opening word: it was a true report she had heard in her own land of Solomon's affairs and his wisdom, and yet, as she will go on to say, she had not believed the words until she came and her own eyes had seen. The two movements belong together — first the report owned true, then the confession that even the report fell short. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the sense of "acts" as words and works alike; Henry reads the verse as the vindication of every soul that will spend and be spent in the pursuit of wisdom; Keil and Delitzsch let the queen's own mounting testimony carry the weight. What all three honour is a rare and honest thing — a great sovereign of the Gentiles, having crossed the desert on a rumour, standing in Solomon's court to declare that the rumour had told the truth, and had told less than the truth. The report that drew her was no lie; the reality outran it, as the following verse will confess more fully still.
6:
"Howbeit I believed not their words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it: and, behold, the one half of the greatness of thy wisdom was not told me: for thou exceedest the fame that I heard." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks how the chronicler has expanded the queen's confession. Where the older account had her say simply, "the half was not told me," our writer makes an explanatory addition, "the one half of the greatness of thy wisdom," using a favourite noun that means increase or multitude and occurs several times in the Chronicles. The closing clause, "thou exceedest the fame," is literally "thou hast added to the report" — Kings has it more fully, "thou hast added wisdom and prosperity to the report." The chronicler, that is, has narrowed the queen's wonder to the one thing he most cares for, the greatness of Solomon's wisdom.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the confession as the highest praise a witness can give — that the reality outran the rumour. She had not believed the words she heard until her own eyes convinced her, and even then she found that report, generous as it was, had understated the truth by half. Henry loves the honesty of it: she came prepared to be disappointed, having discounted the tales as travellers' exaggeration, and instead she is overwhelmed. It is the experience, he would say, of every soul that comes at last to the wisdom of God — that fame falls short of fruition, that the half was not told, that the substance exceeds all that was ever said of it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, still following the queen's speech, set the verse in its place as the summit of her testimony: she had not credited the words until she came and saw, and then the seeing shamed her unbelief. The witnesses converge on the single point that the verse turns on the gap between hearing and seeing. Ellicott shows how the chronicler sharpened that gap to fall upon Solomon's wisdom above all; Henry draws the devotional edge, that the best things are always better in the enjoying than in the report; Keil and Delitzsch let the queen's own words carry their weight. Between them the verse becomes a small parable of faith outdone by sight — a doubter who came to test a rumour and left confessing that no rumour could have done the truth justice, that the king's wisdom was a greatness no messenger had been able to measure or convey.
7:
"Happy are thy men, and happy are these thy servants, which stand continually before thee, and hear thy wisdom." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the queen's beatitude as a lesson on the blessing of good company. Those are happy, he says, who have the opportunity of a constant converse with such as are knowing, wise, and good; the queen thought Solomon's servants happy who continually heard his wisdom, for it seems that even to them he was communicative, and not to visiting royalty alone. Henry adds a striking after-note from the later history: the posterity of those who held places in Solomon's court were content to have the names of their own ancestors forgotten, and thought themselves sufficiently dignified when they were called simply "the children of Solomon's servants," as the returning lists have them in Ezra 2:55 and Nehemiah 7:57. So eminent were those servants that it was honour enough, generations later, to be named from them.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording and hears a small loss of art in it. The verse, as the chronicler gives it, reads "and happy... and hear," with conjunctions that are not found in the older text; and these added particles, Ellicott judges, weaken the rhetorical effect of the queen's exclamation. In Kings the beatitude falls in short, unlinked clauses — "Happy thy men, happy these thy servants" — with the abruptness that belongs to strong feeling; the chronicler's smoother, conjoined phrasing tames it. It is a nicety, but a telling one, showing the later writer's hand even where he follows his source most closely, and reminding the reader that these are two recensions of one speech, not one report set down twice.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott weighs the queen's rhetoric and marks where a copyist's conjunctions blunted it; Henry weighs the queen's meaning and finds in it a benediction on all who live within earshot of wisdom. Between them the verse yields both its form and its force. The queen, dazzled by the king, turns to envy his attendants — not his wealth but his servants seem to her the truly blessed, because they stand continually before him and drink in his wisdom day by day. And Henry will not let the reader miss the application: if it was happiness to hear Solomon, how much more to attend upon a greater than Solomon, and to stand continually before the throne of the wisdom of God.
8:
The queen's praise rises at last from the king to the King's God — "Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee to set thee on his throne, to be king for the LORD thy God" — and the witnesses gather around that daring phrase, "his throne." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the pronoun: it is Jehovah's throne on which Solomon sits, exactly as the Chronicler had said before that the LORD chose Solomon "to sit upon the throne of the kingdom of the LORD" in 1 Chronicles 28:5. Where the older account read "the throne of Israel," the chronicler writes "his throne" and adds the clause "to be king for the LORD thy God" — a further insistence, Ellicott says, on the idea that Solomon was but the vicegerent of Jehovah, holding a delegated crown. He defends the addition against the charge of being a wilful alteration; it is the chronicler's settled theology, not a tampering, and the phrase "to establish them for ever," wanting in the Hebrew of Kings, is probably original, since the Septuagint there preserves it.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the same phrase devotionally. "For the LORD" means in the Lord's name and stead, in a special manner, because Solomon sat in God's own throne and ruled over God's peculiar people, maintaining the honour of God in his land and in the eyes of all the world. The throne of Israel was, in a way no other throne could claim, the throne of God on earth, and its occupant a steward answerable to heaven.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in the queen's blessing a duty for every onlooker. We ought to rejoice and give God thanks for the gifts, graces, and usefulness of others; the queen blessed God for the honour he had put upon Solomon and the favour he had done to Israel in advancing him to the throne. By giving God the praise of the prosperity of others we share in the comfort of it, whereas by envying it we lose the comfort even of our own. And she traces the whole happiness of king and kingdom up to its fountain, the divine favour: it was "because thy God delighted in thee," and "because he loved Israel." Those mercies, Henry says, are doubly sweet in which we can taste the kindness and good will of God as our God. The witnesses close ranks. Ellicott and Wesley establish that Solomon's throne is God's, held in trust; Henry hears a pagan queen preaching to Israel the very doctrine Israel most needed — that the king is God's gift, the kingdom God's love, and every good in both a reason to bless the LORD.
9:
"And she gave the king an hundred and twenty talents of gold, and of spices great abundance, and precious stones: neither was there any such spice as the queen of Sheba gave king Solomon." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works over the wording of the gift. The "spices" are the fragrant balsams from which our very words balsam and balm descend; "great abundance" is again the chronicler's favourite term, here set in place of the older word; and the last clause, "neither was there any such spice," he would render "there had not been such spicery" — that is, in Jerusalem. A defect in the chronicler's manuscript authority, Ellicott suggests, probably occasioned this deviation from the fuller phrase of the older text, "there came no more such abundance of spices."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, reckoning at the chapter's end the value of these royal treasures, computes the queen's hundred and twenty talents of gold at a staggering sum — near nine hundred thousand pounds by his careful arithmetic — so that her present alone was the tribute of a kingdom, laid at the feet of a king she had crossed the desert to honour. The gift was not payment but homage, the overflow of a wonder that could not keep silent and would not come empty-handed.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the exchange of presents that runs through these verses as a lesson in the generosity of the wise. It becomes those who are wise and good, he says, to be generous according to their place and power; the queen was so to Solomon, and Solomon was so to her. Both of them knew how to value wisdom, and therefore neither was covetous of mere money, but each used it to cultivate acquaintance and confirm friendship by mutual gifts. Her gold and spices were the language of esteem, not the price of what she carried home. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the text and the sense of "spices" and "abundance"; Clarke weighs the sheer magnitude of the gold; Henry draws the moral, that liberality is the natural dress of a soul that prizes wisdom above wealth. And the very extravagance of the spicery — a fragrance such as Jerusalem had never known — becomes, in their common reading, a witness to the greatness it honoured: the queen poured out the best her country could yield, because she had found in Solomon a wisdom worth more than all of it, and would not let so rare a treasure be thanked with a small thing.
10:
The narrative breaks from the queen to the wider commerce that fed Solomon's magnificence — "the servants also of Huram, and the servants of Solomon, which brought gold from Ophir, brought algum trees and precious stones." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes first how the chronicler has recast his source. Where the older account spoke of "the fleet of Hiram which carried gold from Ophir," our writer speaks of the servants of Huram and of Solomon, altering the phrase to match his earlier notice of the joint venture in 2 Chronicles 8:18. The gold of Ophir came not by Israelite seamanship alone but by the partnership of Tyre, whose sailors manned the ships and whose king lent his craft to Solomon's enterprise.

The greater part of Ellicott's interest falls on the mysterious "algum trees," a word he had already met at 2 Chronicles 2:8. The Septuagint calls it pine-wood, the Vulgate thyine-wood, the Syriac a kind of acacia; the older account has "almug trees in great abundance." In the Mishnah the cognate word means coral, and the Rabbis ascribe a red colour to the algum; the red sandalwood of India, fragrant and streaked with black, is used both in works of art and for burning, and answers well to the description, while others take it for a white or yellow sandalwood. Thenius, Ellicott reports, doubted whether it was not rather the teak of the East Indies, a hard, yellow-streaked, strongly-scented wood used in India for the building of temples. The exact tree cannot now be named with certainty, but it was plainly a precious, aromatic timber, fetched from far and prized for beauty.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts his eye from the cargo to what it reveals of a nation at ease. These curious imports, he observes, show how prosperity drew the minds of Solomon and his subjects to the love of things curious and uncommon, though useless in themselves; and he draws the sober contrast that true wisdom and happiness are always united together, whereas no such alliance exists between wealth and the enjoyment of the things of this life. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott chases the algum through the versions and the naturalists, and fixes the Tyrian partnership behind the Ophir trade; Henry hears in the very rarity of the freight a quiet warning, that the more a people has of the exotic and the ornamental the less, often, it has of the solid good that only wisdom brings. The gold and the strange sweet timber came home in abundance; but abundance, Henry reminds us, is not the same thing as rest for the soul.
11:
The precious timber fetched from Ophir is now put to use, and the chronicler records it — "the king made of the algum trees terraces to the house of the LORD, and to the king's palace, and harps and psalteries for singers: and there were none such seen before in the land of Judah." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the disputed word "terraces." It is a term that usually means highways, that is, raised or causewayed paths, and it stands here as an interpretation of a rarer word in the older account which occurs only once and whose sense was uncertain; the Septuagint renders it ascents or stairs, the Vulgate steps, the Arabic pillars. Whatever the precise construction, the algum was worked into some raised approach or stairway for the temple and the palace, and into instruments for the temple choir — the harps and psalteries of "the singers," a body dear to the chronicler's heart.

Ellicott hears one phrase ring with the writer's own age and standpoint. "There were none such seen before in the land of Judah" is a shortened paraphrase of the older "there came no such algum trees, nor were seen unto this day"; and that little substitution, "the land of Judah," betrays how utterly the northern kingdom had passed out of the redactor's thought. He writes long after the division and the fall of the north, for whom Judah alone is now the land; the very geography of his praise is the geography of the survivors.

The single witness does not divide against himself, yet the verse rewards the attention he brings to it. The strange sweet wood, carried half the world over, was not hoarded as a curiosity but built into the house of God and shaped into instruments for his praise. The chronicler, ever the temple's historian, records first the stairways of the sanctuary and then the harps of the singers, as though the whole traffic of Ophir found its proper end in worship — the rarest timber of the nations laid under the feet of those who went up to the LORD, and voiced in the music of his courts. Ellicott fixes the philology and recovers the writer's post-exilic vantage; what the verse leaves standing is the picture of a wealth at last well spent, the produce of far-off mines and forests gathered home to adorn the place where God had set his name, so that even Solomon's imports served, in the end, the singing of his praise.
12:
The queen's visit closes with a royal exchange whose exact sense the witnesses find genuinely obscure — "And king Solomon gave to the queen of Sheba all her desire, whatsoever she asked, beside that which she had brought unto the king. So she turned, and went away to her own land, she and her servants." The knot is in the clause "beside that which she had brought." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reasons that it can hardly mean Solomon returned her the very presents she had given him and he had accepted; rather she had asked for various things peculiar to the land of Judea, curiosities in her own kingdom, and besides granting these he gave her other valuable presents of his royal bounty, as the older account states plainly at 1 Kings 10:13. John Wesley reads it the same way: "besides" what he gave her of his royal bounty, which was in compensation for the presents she had brought.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the difficulty bare. It can scarcely mean Solomon gave back her own gifts; if the reading is sound we must understand return-presents equal in value to those she had bestowed, or better regard the clause as a parenthetic note of the chronicler's, to the effect that the giving was not all on one side — Solomon showed himself as royally generous as his visitor. Bertheau, however, would alter the Hebrew slightly to get the sense "beside what the king had brought for her." Ellicott finds the older text much clearer, and notes that the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Syriac each render the clause differently, a sure sign that it was felt to be hard even by the ancients.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry passes over the crux to the courtesy it records. Solomon and the queen both knew how to value wisdom, and therefore neither was covetous of money, but each confirmed their friendship by mutual gifts; the exchange was the seal of an acquaintance contracted in the love of understanding. The witnesses do not finally divide. Clarke and Wesley take the clause of Solomon's bounty going far beyond what the queen had brought; Ellicott weighs whether the text speaks of equal return-gifts or should be lightly emended after Bertheau; Henry reads the whole as generosity meeting generosity. What all agree upon is the picture the verse leaves: a satisfied queen turning homeward laden, having received from Solomon far more than she gave, and carrying back to her own land not gold only but the memory of a wisdom that had exceeded every report — the true gift, which no camel could hold.
13:
"Now the weight of gold that came to Solomon in one year was six hundred and threescore and six talents of gold" — and with this figure the account turns from the queen to the king's own glory. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes only that the verse coincides word for word with the older record at 1 Kings 10:14, the chronicler here copying his source exactly. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, reckoning the sum at the chapter's end, computes these six hundred and sixty-six talents of yearly revenue at nearly five million pounds — an annual income of gold such as no throne of the age could rival.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who reads the whole splendour that this verse opens. Here, he says, is Solomon reigning in wealth and power, in ease and fulness, such as could never since be paralleled by any king. The most illustrious of the great princes were famed for their wars, whereas Solomon reigned forty years in profound peace; some kept their people in awe by distance and seclusion, but Solomon went abroad and appeared in public business. So the promise was fulfilled that God would give him riches and wealth and honour such as no kings had had or should have, the very grant made in 2 Chronicles 1:12. Never any prince had greater plenty of gold and silver, though there were no mines in his own kingdom; either he mastered the mines of other lands or exported the produce of a fruitful country and fetched home this gold in exchange.

Henry lifts the glory toward its antitype. The lustre in which Solomon appeared was typical of the spiritual glory of the kingdom of the Messiah, and but a faint representation of his throne, which is above every throne; yet all Solomon's pomp was artificial, and therefore our Saviour prefers before it the natural beauty of the lilies of the field, for "Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these," as Matthew 6:29 declares. And Henry marks a further mystery in the record: that no mention is here made of Solomon's later departure from God, not the least hint of it. The Holy Ghost, he thinks, would teach us not to take delight in repeating the faults of others, and would intimate that a sin repented of and pardoned is cast behind God's back and remembered no more, as Ezekiel 33:16 promises. The witnesses meet without strain. Ellicott and Clarke fix the figure and its immensity; Henry reads in the golden revenue both the fulfilment of God's word and a shadow of Christ's kingdom, and hears in the chronicler's silence about the king's fall the accent of a mercy that covers what it has forgiven.
14:
"Beside that which chapmen and merchants brought. And all the kings of Arabia and governors of the country brought gold and silver to Solomon." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the opening phrase difficult and probably corrupt. Literally it seems to run "beside the men of the itinerants, and that which the merchants were bringing" — a strange expression, the last word perhaps a clerical error since it recurs directly after. Thenius, he reports, would read instead of "men of the travellers" a word meaning "fines" or "tributes of the subjects," a conjecture the ancient versions seem to bear out: the Syriac of Chronicles has "tributes of the cities," and the Septuagint "men of the subjected states." So the traders' traffic may in truth have been the tribute of vassals. "The kings of Arabia," Ellicott adds, appear in the older text as "the kings of the mixed tribes," the Bedouin bordering on and mingling with Israel; the difference depends on the vowel-pointing only. "Governors" is the word for pashas, and Thenius is wrong to make it a token of late composition; "brought" means "used to bring," a habitual, year-by-year tribute.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only the Targum's rendering, which for "the kings of Arabia" reads "the kings of Sistevantha," and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the text forward into the catalogue of golden treasures that follows — the targets and shields, the ivory throne, the vessels of gold — of which this stream of tribute and trade was the source.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the tribute for what it says of Solomon's standing among the nations. Never any prince, he observes, had such presents brought him by all his neighbours: the kings of Arabia and the governors of the country brought him gold and silver, not as tribute extorted by force but as freewill offerings to procure his favour, or in exchange for the produce of his husbandry. Herein, Henry says, he was a type of Christ, to whom, as soon as he was born, the wise men of the east brought their gold and frankincense and myrrh in Matthew 2:11. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott and Keil labour at the corrupt Hebrew and the identity of the traders and kings; Clarke supplies the Targum; Henry lifts the incoming gold into its gospel figure — the nations of the earth, then as now, bringing their treasures to a king whose greatness God had made, a foreshadowing of the greater homage the Gentiles would one day lay at the feet of David's greater Son.
15:
"And king Solomon made two hundred targets of beaten gold: six hundred shekels of beaten gold went to one target." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the verse stands word for word as the older account at 1 Kings 10:16, and turns to the two disputed terms. "Beaten gold" Gesenius took for mixed or alloyed gold, but the word rather means gold beaten out, gold-leaf, as the Septuagint's "hammered" confirms; and the phrase our version renders "went to" means properly "he put on" — that is, he plated the targets with the gold, overlaying them as one puts sackcloth upon the loins in Amos 8:10. The targets themselves were large oblong shields, the greater of the two kinds of defensive armour, and Solomon covered two hundred of them each with six hundred shekels' weight of hammered gold.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, who reserves his careful valuation for the end of the chapter, reckons each target at a fifth of a talent and the two hundred together at some three hundred thousand pounds — a parade of wealth worn not for war but for show, gold shields that could turn no sword but dazzled every eye.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the golden targets as the first item in Solomon's public splendour. Never any prince appeared in public with greater magnificence, he says; the two hundred targets and three hundred shields of beaten gold were carried before him, and he sat upon a most stately throne, so that there was not the like in any kingdom. To those who judge, as most people do, by the sight of the eye, this outward lustre would greatly recommend him. Yet Henry will not let the gold have the last word: all this pomp was artificial, and the lustre in which Solomon shone was but a faint and typical representation of the spiritual glory of the kingdom of the Messiah, whose throne is above every throne. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the metal as beaten gold-leaf and the making as a plating of the great shields; Clarke measures the fortune hung upon them; Henry reads the whole display as at once a marvel to the eye and a parable to the soul — a king armoured in gold he would never fight in, a magnificence real enough to astonish the world and yet only the shadow of a greater kingdom whose glory needs no beaten metal to set it forth.
16:
"And three hundred shields of beaten gold: three hundred shekels of gold went to one shield. And the king put them in the house of the forest of Lebanon." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott distinguishes this second, lesser piece of armour from the target of the verse before. The shield here is the round or oval buckler, about half the size of the great target with which it is so often contrasted, as in Psalms 35:2, where the Septuagint sets the two side by side.

The weight of gold assigned to each shield raises a genuine difficulty of the text. Our verse says "three hundred shekels," but the older account reads "three manehs of gold"; and the maneh or mina was a sixtieth part of a talent, equivalent to fifty or sixty shekels, so that three manehs would be far more than three hundred shekels. Either, Ellicott reasons, the reading of our text is an error of transcription, the words for "three hundred" and "three manehs" standing close in Hebrew, or else the word "shekels" is wrongly supplied by our translators and we should rather read "three hundred drachms," a hundred drachms making one mina. The Syriac, he notes, reads "three minas of gold wrought on the handle of one shield," and the Arabic agrees; the ancient witnesses themselves felt the figure to be uncertain.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, gathering the targets and shields together, reads them as the moving parade of Solomon's glory — three hundred golden bucklers to go with the two hundred golden targets, all borne before the king, and then laid up in that great hall of state, the house of the forest of Lebanon, so named for its rows of cedar pillars. There was not the like, Henry says, in any kingdom; yet he keeps his settled caution over all this magnificence, that it was but the artificial lustre of a throne whose true glory lay in the wisdom God had given, and that the pomp which so recommends a prince to the eye of the world is at best the shadow of the kingdom that cannot be shaken. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott sorts the smaller shield from the greater target and weighs, with the versions, whether the gold on each was three hundred shekels or three minas; Henry sees the whole golden armoury borne in procession and stored in the cedar hall, and reads it, like all Solomon's splendour, as a magnificence real to the eye but shadowy beside the substance it was made to foreshadow.
17:
"Moreover the king made a great throne of ivory, and overlaid it with pure gold." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott touches the wording with his usual economy: "pure" is here the common Hebrew word for clean or pure gold, standing in place of a rare and once-occurring term used in the older account — one more instance of the chronicler quietly modernizing a difficult word as he copies. The throne itself was framed of ivory and then sheathed in gold, so that the white and the gold together made a seat of state unmatched in the world.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, fascinated as always by oriental lore, refers the reader to a curious description of Solomon's throne which he reproduces at the chapter's end from a Persian manuscript — a legendary account in which the throne is the work of a genie, mounted by seven steps flanked with golden lions and jewelled trees full of mechanical birds that sang and fluttered as the king ascended, until at his reaching the topmost step the whole throne, with all its birds and beasts, was set in motion and voices praised the God of David. Clarke relays the tale not as sober history but as a specimen of how the eastern writers exalted Solomon beyond all bounds, crediting him with command over demons and the very speech of birds; the fable is worth noting only as the shadow that the real magnificence cast in the imagination of later ages, a measure of how deeply the memory of Solomon's throne had stamped itself on the East.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the throne among the marvels that made Solomon's court a wonder to every visitor. He sat, Henry says, upon a most stately throne, such that there was not the like in any kingdom; and this outward grandeur, dazzling to the sight, was typical of the spiritual glory of the kingdom of the Messiah, and but a faint representation of his throne, which is above every throne. The ivory spoke of a purity, and the gold of a worth, that the earthly seat could only shadow. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the plain sense — a great ivory throne overlaid with pure gold, the chronicler smoothing an old word as he writes; Clarke gathers the extravagant legend that grew up around it; Henry reads the actual throne as a type, a shadow cast forward of the throne of the Son of David whose kingdom has no end. The three readings sit well together: the sober text, the fabulous embroidery of the East, and the sober gospel meaning — for the throne that so amazed the queen and bred such legends was, in Henry's eye, only a footstool beside the throne it was made to prefigure.
18:
The throne is described in detail — "there were six steps to the throne, with a footstool of gold, which were fastened to the throne, and stays on each side of the sitting place, and two lions standing by the stays." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes his attention on the golden footstool, and finds the text here suspect. Instead of the chronicler's "a footstool of gold, which were fastened to the throne," the older account reads, "and the throne had a rounded top behind." Although the footstool is a prominent object in oriental representations of thrones, Ellicott judges it quite possible that our text is due to a corruption of the reading that appears in Kings, with which the Syriac here agrees; the Septuagint renders "six steps to the throne, fastened with gold," omitting the footstool altogether, and the Hebrew of our verse is at all events suspiciously awkward. For the rest of the description he refers the reader to the parallel at 1 Kings 10:19, noting that the chronicler has made two slight verbal corrections in the verse that follows.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the throne whole, with its six steps and its armrests and its guardian lions, as part of the state in which Solomon appeared before the world. He sat upon a most stately throne, Henry says, such that there was not the like in any kingdom; and this magnificence, so imposing to the eye, was typical of the spiritual glory of the kingdom of the Messiah, and a faint shadow of that throne which is above every throne. The lions that stood by the stays and ranged upon the steps — the emblem, in Israel, of the tribe of Judah and of the royal house of David — gave the seat a majesty that matched the king's renown.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott weighs the letter, and inclines to think the golden footstool of our text a corruption of the "rounded top" of the older record, the reading uncertain among the versions; Henry weighs the whole, and reads the towering, lion-flanked throne as the outward sign of a wisdom and honour God had given, and as a type reaching forward to a greater King. Between the grammatical care and the typological eye the verse yields both its difficulty and its meaning: a seat of ivory and gold, mounted by six steps and guarded by lions, unmatched in any kingdom of the earth — and yet, for all its grandeur, only the shadow of the throne of the Son of David, before whom the true homage of the nations would one day be laid.
20:
"And all the drinking vessels of king Solomon were of gold, and all the vessels of the house of the forest of Lebanon were of pure gold: none were of silver; it was not any thing accounted of in the days of Solomon." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott looks to the last clause and defends our version's supplied negative. The words "none were of silver" rightly render the sense, he says, comparing the parallel at 1 Kings 10:21, with which the verse otherwise entirely agrees: silver was so common in Solomon's reign that it counted for nothing, and no cup or vessel of the royal service was made of a metal so cheap. The gold plate of the palace and of the great cedar hall was matched by no silver at all, for silver had sunk beneath the notice of a court awash in gold.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads that studied disregard of silver as a window into the vanity of very wealth. Never any prince, he observes, had greater plenty of gold and silver, so great that silver was nothing accounted of; and here Henry sounds his sober note over all this abundance. True wisdom and happiness, he says, are always united together, but no such alliance exists between wealth and the enjoyment of the things of this life. A king might drink from nothing but gold and reckon silver beneath him, and be no nearer rest of soul for it; the very surfeit that made silver worthless could not purchase the one thing needful. Let us then, Henry urges, acquaint ourselves with the Saviour, that we may find rest for our souls where no heap of gold can give it.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the plain sense — that silver was so abundant as to be despised, the palace served wholly in gold — and Henry hears in that very despising of silver the emptiness that dwells at the heart of riches. Between them the verse becomes more than an inventory of plate. It is a picture of a wealth so vast that a precious metal had become contemptible, and, in Henry's reading, a quiet sermon on the poverty of all such abundance: for the court where gold was common and silver scorned was still a court of dying men, and the surpassing riches of Solomon, real as they were and given of God, could no more satisfy the soul than the silver he had learned to disdain.
21:
"For the king's ships went to Tarshish with the servants of Huram: every three years once came the ships of Tarshish bringing gold, and silver, ivory, and apes, and peacocks." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the vexed phrase "the king's ships went to Tarshish." The older account read that "the king had a Tarshish fleet on the sea, with the fleet of Hiram" — a "Tarshish fleet" meaning ships built for long voyages, as the term is used in Isaiah 2:16; and it is generally assumed that the chronicler's words are an erroneous paraphrase of that expression, turning a class of ship into a destination. Yet Ellicott is not sure the assumption is safe. Solomon may in fact have had a fleet in the Mediterranean — "the sea" of the older text — trading westward, as well as his fleet in the Red Sea trading south and east, so that ships that really went to Tarshish are not impossible; and he notes that the identity of this fleet with the Ophir venture named earlier in the chapter is by no means evident. Some, he adds, would place Tarshish at Cape Tarsis in the Persian Gulf.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke records the Targum's gloss, which for "went to Tarshish" reads simply "went to Africa," a paraphrase that shows how uncertain the old geographers were of the place. The commodities the ships brought home — gold and silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks — are the marks of a far and exotic traffic, the wealth and the curiosities of distant coasts.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees in the triennial voyages the enterprise of a wise prince who made the seas serve his throne. Solomon exported the produce of his fruitful country and fetched home in exchange the gold and rarities of the world, so that his navy was one of the engines of the plenty that filled his reign. Yet the apes and peacocks among the freight belong, in Henry's larger reading, to those curious and uncommon things that prosperity taught Solomon and his people to love, precious to the eye and useless to the soul. The witnesses do not divide sharply. Ellicott weighs whether "ships to Tarshish" is the chronicler's slip or a real westward fleet, and refuses to settle it dogmatically; Clarke marks the old confusion over Tarshish itself; Henry reads the whole traffic as at once the fruit of Solomon's God-given wealth and a gentle warning, that a nation may fill its harbours with gold and its palaces with peacocks and still want the one treasure that abides.
22:
"And king Solomon passed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the verse its plain force: "passed all" means "was great above all," and the words answer to the older summary at 1 Kings 10:23. It is the chronicler's crowning estimate of the reign — not that Solomon was among the great kings of his age, but that he stood above them all, and in the two things that are named together, riches and wisdom, as though the wealth were the visible seal of the wisdom, and the wisdom the true root of the wealth.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads that supremacy as the fulfilment of a promise and the pattern of a greater King. Never any prince, he says, was so renowned for wisdom, so courted, so consulted, so admired; and never any so prospered, so that the word given at the outset of the reign, that God would make him great above all, was made good to the letter. Yet Henry's eye travels past Solomon to the Son of David whom he prefigures. The kings of the earth sought to hear Solomon's wisdom, but in a greater than Solomon are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; and it is the shame of the princes of this world that, while they crossed deserts to hear an earthly sage, they will not seek the wisdom of God in Christ, to whom the things of the Spirit are foolishness, as 1 Corinthians 2:8 laments. So the very greatness that drew the nations to Solomon becomes, in Henry's hands, a reproach to a world that neglects a greater treasure freely offered.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the sense — Solomon was great above all the kings of the earth, in wealth and wisdom together — and refers it to its source in the older record; Henry reads that unrivalled eminence as at once the keeping of God's word to the young king and a type of the surpassing greatness of Christ, in whom wisdom and riches meet in a fulness no earthly throne could hold. Between them the brief verse becomes the hinge of the chapter's praise: all that has been told of gold and thrones and navies and tribute is gathered into this single verdict, that Solomon outshone every king on earth; and all that verdict foreshadows is gathered into Henry's reminder that the wisest and richest of men was, after all, but the shadow of one greater, whose wisdom the kings of the earth still too often refuse to seek.
23:
"And all the kings of the earth sought the presence of Solomon, to hear his wisdom, that God had put in his heart." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the chronicler's "all the kings of the earth" is explanatory of the older phrase, "all the earth were seeking Solomon," the vaguer expression defined and made concrete: it was the kings, the great ones, who came, "the earth" here meaning the world of monarchs later bounded in the verses that follow. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the sweep of the words with a sober qualification — "all the kings of the earth" means all in those parts of the world, the princes within reach of Solomon's fame, not literally every ruler under heaven. Keil and Delitzsch carry the text forward through the presents the kings brought and the horses and chariots Solomon kept, gathering these closing verses into one general estimate of his greatness drawn together from the older records.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens on the phrase "that God had put in his heart" and on the dignity of the seekers. The kings of the earth sought to hear Solomon's wisdom, he observes — for it was too great a favour for common persons to pretend to — his natural philosophy, his skill in the conduct of life, perhaps the principles of his religion and the reasons of it. And the sight of those monarchs travelling to sit at the feet of a wise man aggravates, shames, and condemns the general contempt of Christ and his gospel; for in Christ are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, yet none of the princes of this world desire to know them, since they are foolishness to the natural man, as 1 Corinthians 2:14 declares. That Solomon's wisdom was God's own gift, "put in his heart," makes the seeking of it both fitting and a rebuke to those who will not seek the greater wisdom of God in his Son.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott and Keil fix the reach of the phrase and the chronicler's summarizing method; Wesley trims "all the kings" to all in those parts; Henry lifts the whole scene into its gospel light. What binds them is the source named in the verse itself — the wisdom the kings crossed the world to hear was not Solomon's achievement but God's deposit in his heart, and the homage of the nations to that gift is, in Henry's reading, a standing witness against every later age that has a greater Wisdom near at hand and will not so much as seek its presence.
24:
"And they brought every man his present, vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and raiment, harness, and spices, horses, and mules, a rate year by year." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the habitual force of the verbs and the sense of two words. "They brought" means "used to bring," a recurring, customary homage, as the like phrase in the earlier tribute verse; "harness" is not gear for horses but weapons and armour, the old English word Shakespeare keeps in Macbeth's "we'll die with harness on our back"; and "a rate year by year" is literally "a year's matter in a year," the fixed annual due of Solomon's vassal kings. These were not casual gifts but a settled tribute, rendered season upon season by the princes who owned his supremacy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the yearly stream of presents as the homage of the nations to a king whom God had made great, and lifts it at once into its gospel figure. Never any prince had such presents brought him by all his neighbours; and herein Solomon was a type of Christ, to whom, as soon as he was born, the wise men of the east brought their gold and frankincense and myrrh in Matthew 2:11, and to whom all who are about him must still bring their gifts. Henry hears in the tribute of silver and gold, raiment and spices and horses, a foreshadowing of the day when the kings of the earth would bring their glory to a greater throne, and when every believer would present himself a living sacrifice, the reasonable service of Romans 12:1.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school as Ellicott, carry the verse within their summary of the reign's magnificence, treating the annual tribute as one item in the general estimate of the wealth and dominion that God's promise had brought the king. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the customary, yearly nature of the homage and the meaning of "harness" as armour; Keil sets it in the sum of Solomon's greatness; Henry reads the incoming gifts as the type of a homage the nations would one day bring to Christ. Between the grammatical care and the typological eye the verse yields both its history and its hope — a world of kings sending their yearly tribute to Jerusalem, and, behind that annual procession of gifts, the promise of a greater ingathering, when the treasures of the Gentiles would be laid at the feet of David's greater Son.
25:
"And Solomon had four thousand stalls for horses and chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen; whom he bestowed in the chariot cities, and with the king at Jerusalem." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the number against the parallels and finds a real divergence. Our verse gives four thousand stalls, but the passage in 1 Kings 4:26 states the number at forty thousand, which Ellicott judges an error; the remainder of the verse coincides with the older account of the chariot cities. He observes the chronicler's method too: having already given a full account of Solomon's chariots and horses, and his importing of them from Egypt, in the opening chapter at 2 Chronicles 1:14, an account identical with the older record, the writer naturally avoids merely repeating that passage here, and contents himself with this brief notice.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke likewise refers the reader to his note on the discrepant numbers at 1 Kings 4:26, and records that the Targum, in place of four thousand, reads four hundred — a third figure among the witnesses to the count. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school with Ellicott, note that this verse does not correspond to the horse-trade passage of the older Kings but agrees in substance and language with an earlier notice of Solomon's chariots, the chronicler drawing his materials from more than one place in the older histories and setting them here in a single summary. The four thousand stalls, the twelve thousand horsemen, the chariots distributed among the fortress-cities and the guard at Jerusalem, make up the standing military establishment of a reign otherwise famed for peace — a force kept less for war than for the security and the state of a king who ruled from the river to the border of Egypt.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance — a vast establishment of horses, chariots, and horsemen, quartered partly in the fortified chariot cities and partly with the king at Jerusalem — but they weigh the number differently, and none pretends the figures are easily harmonized. Ellicott takes four thousand for the true reading and forty thousand for a slip of the older text; Clarke marks the Targum's four hundred; all agree that the chronicler, having told the story of Solomon's cavalry once already, here only gathers it into a summary. And beneath the arithmetic lies the shadow the next verses will not let the reader forget — that a king of Israel was expressly forbidden to multiply horses to himself, so that this splendid stable, real sign of Solomon's power, was also the first visible breach of the law laid on his throne, a warning the closing verses will make plain.
26:
"And he reigned over all the kings from the river even unto the land of the Philistines, and to the border of Egypt." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that this verse corresponds to the summary of Solomon's dominion at 1 Kings 4:21, and that "the earth" over whose kings he reigned, spoken of vaguely in the verses before, is here defined by its bounds — from the Euphrates, "the river" by eminence, across to the Philistine coast and down to the frontier of Egypt. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, reckon the verse among the notices the chronicler has drawn together at the reign's close, and cross-reference it under the Hebrew numbering to the parallel they cite as 1 Kings 5:1 — the very verse Ellicott names under the English numbering, the two witnesses pointing to one text under the differing versification of the Hebrew and the English Bibles.

The extent so defined is no small thing. Solomon's sway reached from the great river of the north to the border of Egypt in the south, over all the lesser kings between; and the phrase marks the widest compass the kingdom of Israel ever attained, the near approach to the ideal breadth of the promised land. The chronicler, gathering his summary from more than one place in the older records, sets this dominion as the outward frame of all the wealth and wisdom he has been recounting: a king who was not only rich and wise above all others but sovereign over a swathe of the earth from the Euphrates to the Nile. The tribute-bearing kings of the verses before are here shown to be his subjects, not merely his admirers; the gold and the spices and the yearly presents flowed inward because a real supremacy stood behind them, a dominion broad and settled and owned on every side.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the parallel and the defining of "the earth" by its boundaries; Keil sets the verse among the drawn-together notices of the reign and confirms, under another numbering, the same cross-reference. Between them they leave the reader with the full reach of Solomon's peace — a dominion broad and quiet, holding tribute from every king within its bounds, the high-water mark of Israel's greatness under the son of David. It is the last height before the descent; for the chapter that has climbed through gold and thrones and navies to this imperial summary will turn, in a few verses more, to the king's death and the passing of his crown to a son under whom that wide dominion would be torn in two.
27:
"And the king made silver in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar trees made he as the sycomore trees that are in the low plains in abundance." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the verse is identical with the older account at 1 Kings 10:27, and repeats too the earlier hyperbole of the opening chapter; but he adds a pointed cross-reference, that on this verse and the next the reader should compare the royal prohibitions of Deuteronomy 17:16 and following — the law that forbade the king of Israel to multiply to himself silver and gold, or horses, or wives. The abundance so gloried in here stands, in Ellicott's eye, under the shadow of that ancient statute, and the wealth that made silver as common as street-stones was the very heaping-up the law had warned against.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the extravagant figure into a lesson on the two kinds of treasure. Solomon made silver and gold so plentiful in Jerusalem that he made them cheap and common as the stones of the road; and here, Henry says, lies the great difference between earthly and heavenly riches. The increase of gold lowers the value of it, so that the more men have the less each piece is worth; but the increase of grace advances its price, for the more men have of grace the more they prize it. How much better, therefore, is it to get wisdom than gold — the wisdom Solomon had asked before ever the wealth was added, and which no abundance could cheapen as abundance had cheapened his silver.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the identity of the verse with its parallel and sets the Deuteronomic prohibition quietly beside it, so that the reader feels the law's warning under the boast of plenty; Henry weighs the boast itself on the scales of the sanctuary and finds that the very thing which makes gold worthless in abundance is what makes grace more precious the more one has of it. Between them the verse becomes two-edged: a dazzling picture of a capital where silver lay like gravel and cedar was as cheap as the common sycomore of the lowlands, and beneath it a double caution — the king's heaping of treasure trespassing on a plain command of God, and the soul reminded that the wisest wealth is the wisdom which grows dearer, never cheaper, the more richly it is possessed.
28:
"And they brought unto Solomon horses out of Egypt, and out of all lands." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verbs and the reach of the trade. "They brought" is again the habitual form, "used to bring"; and the verse summarizes the fuller passage of the older account at 1 Kings 10:28, the same traffic already told in the opening chapter, adding here that Solomon imported horses not from Egypt only but "out of all lands." Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, had noted in their survey of these closing verses that the whole description is here rounded off, all that had been said earlier about the horse-trade with Egypt being drawn together into one general statement — the chronicler gathering his scattered notices into a single summary of the king's commerce.

Yet the mention of Egypt carries a weight the bare summary does not spell out, and it is the same weight Ellicott had just set beside the verse before, in his cross-reference to the royal prohibitions of the law. For the king of Israel had been expressly forbidden to multiply horses, and above all to cause the people to return to Egypt to multiply them; and this notice, that Solomon's horses came up out of Egypt and out of all lands, records the very traffic that statute had guarded against. The chronicler tells it without comment, as he tells the whole glory of the reign, and passes over in silence the decline to which such commerce would lead — that silence itself, as the puritan reading of an earlier verse observed, being the mercy of an inspired historian who will not rake up a repented sin.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the habitual sense of the verb and the summarizing reach of "all lands"; Keil marks the rounding-off of the description into one general statement. Between them the verse stands as the last note of Solomon's magnificence and the quietest of its warnings: a king whose horses were fetched from Egypt and every land, whose stables and navies and golden thrones had made him great above all the kings of the earth, and yet whose very greatness, in this one forbidden traffic, held the seed of a sorrow the chronicler forbears to name, keeping his record fixed to the end upon the glory God had given rather than the folly the king would add.
29:
"Now the rest of the acts of Solomon, first and last, are they not written in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the visions of Iddo the seer against Jeroboam the son of Nebat?" The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the opening — "the rest of the acts" is literally "the rest of the words," that is, the history — and marks that where our text has "first and last," or "the former and the latter," the older account had "and all that he did, and his wisdom." For the prophetic sources here named he refers to the Introduction, adding a striking note on the king's own name: "Solomon" conveyed the idea of peace to the Hebrew ear, yet there is no doubt it was originally identical with Shalman, an Assyrian divine name, borne, as the monuments show, by a Salamanu king of Moab in the days of Tiglath-pileser. Ahijah the Shilonite he ties to the prophet who foretold the rending of the kingdom in 1 Kings 11:29.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses attend to the sources and their loss. Adam Clarke states plainly that these books of Nathan, Ahijah, and Iddo are all lost, and refers the reader to his review of Solomon's character and works at the close of the older history; the annals from which Scripture drew are gone, and only the inspired record remains. John Wesley adds that Iddo and the other prophets named were also historians, who wrote the annals of their times, out of which these sacred books were taken, whether by the prophets themselves or by others.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in this closing formula the note of a life's vanity and a God's mercy. Here is Solomon dying, stripped of his pomp, and leaving all his wealth and power to one whom he knew would be a fool, a grief that was not only vanity but vexation of spirit, the very complaint of Ecclesiastes 2:19. And Henry marvels that the record makes no mention of Solomon's departure from God in his latter days — not the least hint of it. The Holy Ghost, he thinks, would teach us not to delight in repeating the faults of others, and would signify that a sin repented of and pardoned is cast behind God's back and remembered no more. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the sense and the sources and the curious history of the name; Clarke and Wesley trace the lost prophetic annals behind the sacred books; Henry reads in the bare formula both the emptiness of a glory that must be left to a fool and the tenderness of a God who buries in silence the sins he has forgiven.
30:
"And Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel forty years." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the verse answers exactly to the older record at 1 Kings 11:42, and lays weight on the phrase "over all Israel" — that is, over the undivided nation. It is the last time the words can be used without irony; for the kingdom that Solomon held whole would, within a verse or two of the narrative, be torn in two under his son. Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse plainly as the notice of the length of the reign, joined to the record of the king's death, burial, and successor that follows, drawn from the same close of the older history.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the forty years in the light of the peace that filled them. The most illustrious of the great princes of the earth, he had observed, were famed for their wars, whereas Solomon reigned forty years in profound peace — a reign not of conquest but of quiet, prosperity, and worship, under a king whose very name meant peace. That long tranquillity was itself a mark of the favour of God, the fulfilment of the word that had promised the son of David rest on every side, and the setting in which the temple was built and the wisdom of the throne made known to the nations. And unlike the recluse princes who kept their people in awe by distance, Henry notes, Solomon went abroad and appeared in public business, so that his forty years were not the seclusion of a tyrant but the open reign of a king his people could see and his neighbours could seek.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the parallel and the poignancy of "all Israel," the undivided nation soon to be rent; Keil reads the verse as the sober close of the reign; Henry recalls the forty years of peace as the crown of a reign unlike any other. Between them the brief notice gathers the whole chapter to a point. All the gold and the thrones, the navies and the tribute and the wisdom that drew the kings of the earth, belonged to a reign of forty untroubled years over a people still whole; and the very completeness of that dominion, "over all Israel," sounds the first low note of the division to come — for the next words will tell of Solomon's death, and the crown will pass to a son under whom the one nation would become two, and the golden age here summed up would close.
31:
"And Solomon slept with his fathers, and he was buried in the city of David his father: and Rehoboam his son reigned in his stead." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the death-formula with his usual care for the letter. "Slept" is literally "lay down," the settled biblical idiom for going the way of one's ancestors; and where our text has "he was buried," the Hebrew reads "they buried him," the older account keeping the passive — the two texts, Ellicott notes, being otherwise identical. Keil and Delitzsch likewise take the verse simply as the record of the king's death, his burial in the city of David, and the accession of his son, the close of the reign drawn from the same place in the older history.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley turns the bare obituary into a meditation on the vanity of all earthly glory. Here, he says, is Solomon in his throne, and Solomon in his grave; for the throne could not secure him from the grave. Here is he stripped of his pomp, leaving all his wealth and power — and leaving it not even to one of whom he knew not whether he would prove a wise man or a fool, but to one he knew would be a fool. This, Wesley concludes, was not only vanity but vexation of spirit, the very verdict Solomon himself had passed on such a succession. The wisest and richest of kings must lie down like any man, and hand his glory to an heir who would squander it.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the idiom and the slight change of voice between the two records; Keil reads the plain notice of death and succession; Wesley draws the sermon the verse preaches of itself. Between the grammatical care and the devotional eye the chapter closes on a grave. The king who passed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom sleeps with his fathers in the city of David, his pomp stripped away, his crown passed to Rehoboam, under whom the wide and peaceful dominion just summed up would be broken. The throne could not keep him from the tomb; and the golden reign that filled the chapter ends, as every reign must, in the common dust — a last and sober word that no wealth, no wisdom, and no throne can ward off or prepare a man for the stroke of death, and that only a greater Son of David would rise from it.
